blocks.text: Re: Kickoff, Roadway Widening Study for {{fact:f:E-2005-001.client}}

This memorandum records the assignments and the sequence of work agreed at the kickoff meeting, so that the project file reflects them. Work commences {{fact:f:E-2005-001.start}}. Linda George is project manager and the client contact runs through her. The objective of the study is a defensible recommended section for the corridor, with an alignment good enough to fix the grading limits and the right-of-way, a drainage analysis that stands up at the county counter, and a cost opinion the client can budget against. It is a preliminary engineering study. It is not construction drawings, and nothing in this file should start to look like construction drawings.

James Skinner, General Manager at Evans-Lee, is the client contact. He has the plat, the boundary survey, and the correspondence with the county, and Linda George will contact him for them. Nobody else telephones Evans-Lee about this project. A question that needs an answer from Evans-Lee goes to Linda George and she asks it. This is not ceremony. The client should hear one voice from this firm, and a call from three of us produces three answers and no record of any of them.

items: 1. Record information. Linda George requests the county as-builts for the roadway and the cross drains, and the utility records from each owner. Log every request in the project folder with the date it went out. We have been made to wait for record drawings before, and the log is what proves who is waiting on whom.

2. Field survey and base mapping. Survey ties to county control. I build the base map from the survey and nothing else. If the survey and the as-builts disagree, the survey governs and the disagreement gets noted, not smoothed over.

3. Counts and capacity. Turning-movement and volume counts at the study intersections, ordinary weekdays, school in session. Counts taken while school is out are not the corridor and we will not use them.
4. Alignment and typical section. Linda George sets the horizontal and vertical alignment. I draft the typical section and the exhibits from it. The section changes only when she changes it.
5. Drainage. Linda George's work. I furnish the ditch cross sections and the culvert inventory from the survey.

6. Quantities and cost opinion. Mine. Takeoffs come off the alignment and the section as issued, not off a draft I happened to have open. I check them once forward and once backward, and I would rather hold a number a day than issue one that is wrong.
7. Report. Linda George writes it. I supply the exhibits and the quantity tables in the form she asks for.
Requests for record information go out this week. Nothing else waits on them, but everything else is worth less until they arrive.
Survey crew scheduled once the study limits are fixed on a marked-up aerial. Linda George marks it up; I will not guess at the limits.
Counts ordered after the limits are set, so we count the intersections that are in the study and not the ones that are near it.

Project folder lettered and opened on the file share before any of the above. Correspondence, requests, and the count sheets go in it the day they exist. A document that lives on somebody's desk is not in the file.
Progress meeting with Evans-Lee once the base map and the counts are in hand. Linda George runs it.

blocks.text: One point on the file, and then I am done. Everything that goes to the client or the county on this project is reviewed and sealed before it leaves. That includes the exhibits. If you are not certain a sheet is ready to be seen outside this office, it is not ready, and Linda George would rather be asked than told afterward.
